title: Paying Off Credit Card Debt
prompt_text: Your cousin has called you feeling stressed about a credit card balance that keeps growing because of high interest charges. Every month the minimum payment barely makes a dent, and the debt feels like it will never go away. Your cousin knows you are good with money and wants your honest advice. What advice would you give your cousin about paying off this credit card debt?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Open with empathy, give one clear main strategy (pay more than the minimum and target the highest-interest card first), back it with two reasons, add a short personal example, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Hey, I'm really sorry you're feeling so stressed about this, but honestly, you can totally beat this debt. If I were you, the first thing I'd do is stop using that card completely so the balance doesn't keep climbing. After that, what I'd suggest is paying more than the minimum every single month, even if it's just an extra fifty dollars, because the minimum mostly covers interest and barely touches what you actually owe. On top of that, I'd call the bank and ask if they can lower your interest rate, or look into moving the balance to a card with zero percent interest for the first year. Trust me, that one move can save you hundreds. When I was paying off my own card, I cut out eating lunch out and put that money straight onto the balance, and it disappeared way faster than I expected. At the end of the day, just stay consistent and don't beat yourself up. You've got this, and I'm here if you need help with a plan.

title: Friend Wants To Borrow Money
prompt_text: A close friend of yours has asked to borrow a large sum of money to cover an unexpected expense. You care about this friend, but lending such a big amount makes you uneasy, and you are not sure how to respond without hurting the friendship. Your friend is waiting and is asking you directly for your advice on how to handle this. What would you tell them?
connector_phrases: Another thing to keep in mind
At the end of the day
template_hint: Acknowledge the tough spot, advise protecting both the money and the friendship by setting clear terms (a written agreement and realistic amount), give two reasons, include a brief example, and close warmly.
sample_response_band12: Okay, here's the thing, I know this is a really tricky spot to be in, so let's think it through together. To be honest with you, lending a big chunk of money to a friend can get awkward fast if things aren't crystal clear from the start. What I'd recommend is only lending an amount you'd genuinely be okay never seeing again, just in case, and then putting everything in writing, the amount, the dates, the whole repayment plan. That way nobody's guessing later and the friendship stays protected. Another thing to keep in mind is that it's perfectly fine to say no, or to offer a smaller amount than they asked for. A real friend will understand. My brother once lent a buddy money with a simple signed note, and because the terms were clear, they paid it back and stayed close. At the end of the day, you have to protect both your wallet and the friendship, so just be honest and kind, and trust your gut on this one.

title: Always Overspending Each Month
prompt_text: Your younger sibling just started a full-time job but keeps running out of money before the next paycheque arrives. They are not sure where it all goes and feel embarrassed about overspending every month. They trust your judgment and have asked how they can get their spending under control. What advice would you give your sibling?
template_hint: Reassure your sibling it's common, give one core habit (track every expense to find the leaks), support it with two reasons, share a quick example, and end with confidence.
sample_response_band12: Hey, please don't be embarrassed, almost everyone struggles with this when they get their first real paycheque, so you're totally normal. First of all, what works really well is tracking every single thing you buy for a month, even the small coffees, because you can't fix the problem until you actually see where the money is going. Honestly, I think you'll be shocked how much disappears on little impulse buys. Another thing you could try is moving a set amount into savings the moment you get paid, before you can spend it. That way you're paying yourself first and you naturally live within your means. Plus, it takes the stress out of those last few days before payday. When I started doing this, I realized I was spending almost two hundred dollars a month just on takeout, and cutting that in half made a huge difference. Believe me, once you have a clear picture, it gets so much easier. All in all, just track it, save first, and you'll have this sorted in no time.

title: Starting An Emergency Fund
prompt_text: A coworker of yours recently had a car repair that wiped out all their cash, and it made them realize they have nothing saved for emergencies. They want to start building a safety net but feel overwhelmed because they're starting completely from zero. Knowing you have managed to save, they've asked you how to begin. What advice would you give your coworker?
connector_phrases: The easiest way to start is
template_hint: Normalize starting from zero, advise saving small automatic amounts into a separate account, give two reasons, add a concrete example of a starter goal, and close with motivation.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, the fact that you've decided to start is already the hardest part, so don't feel overwhelmed, everybody begins at zero. The easiest way to start is to open a separate savings account, one you don't touch, and set up a small automatic transfer right after payday, even just twenty or thirty dollars a week. Keeping it separate matters because if the money sits in your regular account, you'll spend it without thinking. On top of that, automating it means you don't have to rely on willpower, the money just moves on its own. What really helps is having a clear first goal, like saving five hundred dollars, because a small target feels reachable and keeps you motivated. When I started, I aimed for one month of expenses, and seeing that cushion grow honestly took a lot of stress off my shoulders. Little by little, those small amounts really add up. Bottom line, start tiny, make it automatic, and within a few months you'll have a real safety net behind you.

title: Buy A Car Or Keep Saving
prompt_text: Your neighbour is trying to decide whether to buy their first car right now or wait and keep saving for a while longer. They have enough for a basic used car, but buying it would use up most of their savings, and they're torn about the timing. They've asked for your honest take on what they should do. What advice would you give your neighbour?
connector_phrases: One thing to think about is
When it comes down to it
template_hint: Acknowledge the dilemma, lean toward waiting a bit while factoring in the total costs of ownership, give two reasons, offer a concrete example, and close by trusting their judgment.
sample_response_band12: Oh, that's a tough one, but if you ask me, I'd lean toward holding off just a little longer before you buy. The way I see it, draining almost all of your savings on the car itself is risky, because a car isn't just the purchase price. One thing to think about is the ongoing costs: insurance, gas, registration, and surprise repairs can easily run you a few hundred dollars a month. If you spend everything now, you'd have no cushion left for those. Also, waiting a few more months means you can afford a slightly newer, more reliable car that won't break down on you. For example, a friend of mine rushed into buying a cheap car and ended up spending more on repairs in the first year than the car cost. That said, if you genuinely need it for work or commuting right now, that changes things. When it comes down to it, just don't leave yourself with zero savings, and you'll make the right call.

title: Budgeting On A Tight Income
prompt_text: A classmate of yours is living on a very tight income and finds it almost impossible to stick to a budget when money is so limited. They feel discouraged because every plan they make seems to fall apart by the middle of the month. They know you manage your money carefully and have asked you for some practical tips. What advice would you give your classmate?
template_hint: Validate the difficulty, advise covering essentials first with a simple realistic plan and a small buffer, give two reasons, include a practical example, and close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: I get it, sticking to a budget when money is really tight is genuinely hard, so please don't be too discouraged with yourself. What I'd start with is keeping it simple, just list your fixed expenses like rent, bills, and food first, and cover those before anything else. The trick is to be realistic instead of overly strict, because if you cut everything out, the plan feels miserable and you give up by mid-month. So leave yourself a tiny bit of breathing room for small treats. Besides that, I'd look for one or two areas to cut back painlessly, like cooking in bulk or cancelling a subscription you barely use, since small savings really add up when income is limited. Say for instance, planning meals for the week instead of buying food day by day can easily save you forty or fifty dollars. More than anything, give it a couple of months, because budgeting is a skill that gets easier with practice. You're already trying, and that honestly counts for a lot.

title: Cutting An Expensive Shopping Habit
prompt_text: Your roommate has realized they are spending a lot of money every month on subscriptions and online shopping they do not really need. They want to break the habit, but they admit it is hard to resist the constant deals and notifications. They have asked you how you would cut back on something like this. What advice would you give your roommate?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
template_hint: Reassure it's a common trap, advise auditing and cancelling unused subscriptions plus a wait rule before buying, give two reasons, add a quick example, and close supportively.
sample_response_band12: You know what, this is such a common trap, those apps are literally designed to make you spend, so honestly don't feel bad about it at all. The first thing I'd do is sit down and go through every subscription you have and cancel the ones you barely touch, because a bunch of small monthly fees quietly add up to a shocking amount over a year. What really helps me is turning off the sale notifications and unsubscribing from store emails, since you can't really be tempted by a deal you never even see. And another thing, give yourself a rule where you wait two days before buying anything online. Like, for example, I add stuff to my cart and just leave it, and half the time I realize a couple days later that I didn't actually want it. That little pause saves me so much money. In the end, just cancel the extras and slow yourself down before you buy, and trust me, you'll see the difference in your account almost right away.

title: Buying a Home or Renting
prompt_text: Your close friend has rented an apartment for years and has finally saved enough for a down payment. They are torn between buying their first house and staying as a renter, and they are nervous about taking on a mortgage. They call you, feeling stressed, and ask what you think they should do. What advice would you give your friend?
connector_phrases: The main thing to consider is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Open warmly, give one clear recommendation, back it with two reasons (financial stability plus lifestyle fit), add a concrete example, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Hey, I totally get why you're stressed, this is a huge decision and it's normal to feel torn. Honestly, if I were you, I'd take a really close look at how long you plan to stay in the city first. The main thing to consider is that buying only pays off if you stay put for, say, five years or more, because that's when you actually start building equity instead of paying someone else's mortgage. On top of that, owning means you're on the hook for repairs, so I'd make sure you keep a little emergency fund aside. What I'd suggest is sitting down with a mortgage broker and getting pre-approved, just so you know your real budget before you fall in love with a place. For example, my cousin almost overspent until the bank showed her a number she could actually handle. At the end of the day, if the numbers work and you feel settled here, go for it. Whatever you decide, I'm here to help you weigh it out, okay?

title: Co-Signing A Relative's Loan
prompt_text: Your cousin wants to buy a car but their credit history is not strong enough to qualify for the loan on their own. They have asked you to co-sign it, which would make you legally responsible if they ever miss payments. Your cousin phones you, unsure how to move forward, and asks what you think they should do. What advice would you give your cousin?
connector_phrases: If I were in your shoes
Another thing to keep in mind
When it comes down to it
template_hint: Acknowledge the family bond, give one firm recommendation for the cousin, support it with two reasons (the risk to whoever co-signs and protecting the relationship), suggest a concrete safer alternative, then close kindly.
sample_response_band12: Okay, first off, I know how much you want this car, so I totally get why you're stressed about it. But here's the thing, asking someone to co-sign is a really big deal, because if you ever fall behind, that loan lands on them legally, not just on you. If I were in your shoes, I'd slow down before putting anyone else on the hook. The big reason is that one missed payment could wreck their credit score, and that's a heavy thing to put on family. Another thing to keep in mind is that money and family don't always mix, so if things go sideways, it could really strain the relationship. What I'd really recommend is building your own credit first so you can qualify on your own. For example, you could get a starter credit card, pay it off every month, and apply again in six months or so. That said, only you know your situation. When it comes down to it, try to stand on your own two feet first, and lean on family only if you truly have to.

title: A Friend's Risky Investment Tip
prompt_text: Your friend Maya has been telling you about a new investment that promises huge returns very quickly. She is excited and is thinking about putting a big chunk of her savings into it, but something about the deal makes her uneasy. She texts you to ask whether she should go ahead with it. What advice would you give her?
template_hint: Open empathetically, advise caution and research, give two reasons (unrealistic promises and risk to savings), suggest investing only what they can afford to lose, add an example, and close supportively.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, I'm really glad you texted me before jumping in, because that uneasy feeling is worth listening to. If you ask me, anytime someone promises huge returns super fast, that's usually a red flag, it often sounds too good to be true because it is. What I'd do first is slow right down and actually research the company yourself instead of trusting the hype. The main reason is that your savings took years to build, and you don't want to risk your whole nest egg on one exciting tip. Plus, even good investments go up and down, so putting everything in one place is just too risky. On the other hand, if you're really curious, I'd only put in a small amount you could honestly afford to lose, and keep the rest safe and diversified. For example, my coworker tried that with a hyped stock and only risked a tiny bit, so when it dropped he was fine. Bottom line, trust your gut, do your homework, and never bet the farm. You've got this.

title: A Tempting Impulse Purchase
prompt_text: Your younger sibling just got their first paycheque and is about to buy an expensive designer watch on credit, even though they admit they don't really need it. They are excited but a little unsure, so they send you a message asking whether they should go ahead. What advice would you give your sibling?
template_hint: Open with warmth, recommend pausing before buying, give two reasons (debt and possible regret), suggest a waiting period or saving first, add a quick example, then close kindly.
sample_response_band12: Listen, congrats on your first paycheque, that's a big deal and I'm proud of you! But before you buy that watch, can I give you a little honest advice? If I were you, I'd hit pause for at least a week before spending that much. The thing is, you already said you don't really need it, and putting it on credit means you could be paying it off for months, plus interest. My advice would be to sleep on it, because impulse buys usually come with buyer's remorse once the excitement wears off. Also, if you still really want it in a couple of weeks, I'd save up and buy it with cash so it actually feels earned. For example, I once rushed to buy a fancy jacket and barely wore it, total waste of money. All in all, treat yourself, you deserve it, but do it in a way that won't stress your budget. Whatever you choose, I'm just happy you're thinking it through. Talk soon!

title: Splitting Costs With A Roommate
prompt_text: Your friend just moved in with a new roommate, and they are already arguing about how to fairly divide rent, groceries, and utility bills. Your friend feels awkward bringing it up and worries about starting a conflict. They have asked you for help. What advice would you give your friend?
template_hint: Validate the awkwardness, recommend an honest calm conversation, give two reasons (avoiding resentment and clear fairness), suggest a concrete system like an app, add an example, then close encouragingly.
sample_response_band12: First of all, I totally understand why this feels awkward, money talks are never fun, especially with someone you just moved in with. But honestly, the sooner you sort it out, the better. What I'd suggest is sitting down together when you're both relaxed and just being open about it. The key is to agree on a system that feels fair to both of you, because if you don't, little frustrations build up and turn into real resentment. On top of that, having clear ground rules from the start means nobody feels taken advantage of later. I'd also say try a bill-splitting app, that way every grocery run and utility payment gets tracked, so it's not just your word against theirs. For example, my brother and his roommate split rent by room size and shared everything else fifty-fifty, and it worked perfectly. In the end, keep it calm and friendly, frame it as 'let's find what's fair,' not 'you owe me.' You'll feel so much lighter once it's settled.

title: Turning a Hobby Into Business
prompt_text: A coworker of yours bakes amazing cakes as a hobby, and friends keep telling them they should start selling. They are thinking about launching a small side business but feel nervous about losing the joy of baking and managing it alongside their full-time job. Over lunch, they ask you for advice on what they should do. What advice would you give your coworker?
template_hint: Affirm their talent, advise starting small and testing demand, give two reasons (lower risk and protecting their passion), suggest a concrete first step, add an example, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: You know what, your cakes are genuinely incredible, so I'm not surprised people keep telling you to sell them! But I hear you, it's scary to turn something you love into work. If it were me, I wouldn't quit anything or go all in right away. What I'd really do is start small, like taking a few orders from friends on weekends to test whether there's real demand. The big reason is that it keeps your risk low, you're not pouring money into a kitchen or licences before you know it'll work. Another thing is that starting slowly protects the joy, because if you suddenly take on twenty orders a week, you'll burn out fast and resent the thing you used to love. That being said, make sure you price your cakes properly so your time is actually worth it. For example, my neighbour began with just one cake a week and grew from there. When all's said and done, dip your toe in, keep it fun, and let it grow naturally. I really think you've got something special here.

title: Selling Or Repairing Old Car
prompt_text: Your neighbour owns an old car that keeps breaking down, and the latest repair quote is quite expensive. They are unsure whether to spend the money fixing it again or to sell it and put that cash toward a newer vehicle. They catch you in the driveway and ask what they should do. What advice would you give your neighbour?
connector_phrases: If I were in your position
At the end of the day
template_hint: Open friendly, recommend comparing repair cost to the car's value, give two reasons (ongoing breakdowns and money wasted), suggest a mechanic's overall check, add an example, then close supportively.
sample_response_band12: Well, I feel for you, there's nothing more frustrating than a car that keeps letting you down! Here's what I'd do if I were in your position. First, I'd compare that repair quote to what the car is actually worth right now. The thing is, if the fix costs almost as much as the car's resale value, it's probably not worth pouring more money in. Plus, if it keeps breaking down, this likely won't be the last repair, and it turns into a money pit that drains your savings bit by bit. On the flip side, before you decide, I'd ask a trusted mechanic for an honest overall check, just so you know whether this is a one-time fix or a sign of bigger problems coming. For example, my brother kept patching his old truck until he realized he'd spent more on repairs than a used car would've cost. At the end of the day, you deserve reliable transportation that doesn't stress you out. Trust your gut, and let me know how it goes.

title: Choosing Between Two Apartments
prompt_text: Your friend has found two apartments and can't decide which one to rent. The first is bigger and cheaper but a long bus ride from work. The second is small and pricey but right downtown near everything. Your friend asks you which apartment you think is the better choice. What advice would you give?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Open warmly, recommend one apartment clearly, back it with two reasons (cost-vs-commute and lifestyle), add a quick personal example, then close encouragingly.
sample_response_band12: Oh, that's a tough one, but honestly, if I were you, I'd go for the bigger place outside downtown. Here's the thing: the rent is cheaper, so you'd have extra money every month for savings or going out, and the extra space really matters when you're living somewhere full-time. Yeah, the commute is longer, but a bus ride isn't the worst. You can read, listen to podcasts, or just decompress after work. When I moved last year, I picked the cheaper spot a bit farther out, and I never regretted it because that bus time actually became my quiet time. The downtown place sounds exciting, but small apartments get cramped fast, and paying a premium just to be central isn't always worth it. What I'd suggest is doing one trial commute during rush hour before you sign anything, just so you know what you're getting into. At the end of the day, more space and lower rent will make daily life way easier. Trust your gut, you've got this!

title: Landlord Ignoring Repair Requests
prompt_text: Your neighbour has been asking their landlord to fix a leaking pipe for weeks, but the landlord keeps ignoring the messages. The leak is getting worse and your neighbour is frustrated and unsure what to do next. Your neighbour asks you how they should handle the situation. What advice would you give?
connector_phrases: If I were in your shoes
The first thing I'd do is
template_hint: Sympathize briefly, then give a clear two-step plan (document in writing, then escalate), explain why each step protects them, add a short example, and close with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Ugh, that's so frustrating, I totally get why you're upset. If I were in your shoes, the first thing I'd do is put everything in writing. Send the landlord a clear email or text describing the leak and ask for a repair date, because phone calls are easy to deny later, but a written message gives you a paper trail. More importantly, take photos of the leak with dates, so you've got proof of how bad it's getting. If they still ignore you, that's when I'd escalate it. Most cities have a tenant board or housing authority you can contact, and honestly, just mentioning that often gets things moving fast. My cousin had the exact same problem, and the moment she emailed about tenant rights, her sink got fixed within two days. Just to be safe, don't stop paying rent out of frustration, because that can actually backfire on you. So stay calm, keep records, and follow up firmly. You've got every right to a safe home, so stand your ground!

title: Moving To A New City
prompt_text: Your cousin has been offered a great job in a city far away and is feeling nervous about leaving friends and family behind. They really want the role but worry about being lonely and starting over somewhere new. Your cousin asks you whether they should take the job and move. What advice would you give?
connector_phrases: When all is said and done
template_hint: Acknowledge the fear, encourage taking the job, give two reasons (career growth and how loneliness is manageable), share a quick example, and end with warm support.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, I think you should go for it. I know leaving everyone behind feels scary, but if you ask me, a chance like this doesn't come around often, and you'd always wonder "what if" if you turned it down. Career-wise, taking the leap now, while you're open to change, can really set you up for the future. Plus, here's the thing about loneliness: it's temporary. Cities are full of people in the exact same boat, and joining a gym, a hobby group, or just grabbing coffee with coworkers helps you build a new support network surprisingly fast. When my brother moved across the country, he was homesick for about a month, but within half a year he had a whole new circle of friends and never looked back. And keep in mind, family is only a video call away, so you're not really losing anyone. When all is said and done, this move could open so many doors for you. I'm excited for you, take the job!

title: One-Year Lease Or Month-To-Month
prompt_text: Your coworker is about to rent an apartment and can't decide between signing a one-year lease or going month-to-month. The yearly lease is cheaper per month but locks them in, while month-to-month costs more but gives them the freedom to leave anytime. Your coworker asks you which option makes more sense for them. What advice would you give?
template_hint: Frame the choice around their situation, lean toward one option with two reasons (cost stability vs. flexibility), give a quick example tied to job certainty, and close decisively.
sample_response_band12: Okay, this really depends on how settled you feel right now. If it were me, and I knew I was staying in this city for a while, I'd sign the one-year lease. The main reason is cost: locking in a lower rate saves you real money over twelve months, and you also get stability, no surprise rent hikes, no scrambling to move. That said, if your job feels uncertain or you think you might relocate, month-to-month could actually be smarter, even though it's pricier. The flexibility to leave anytime is worth a lot when life is up in the air. Here's something to think about: breaking a one-year lease early can cost you a penalty, sometimes a couple months' rent, so only commit if you're fairly sure you'll stay. A friend of mine signed a yearly lease, then got transferred, and paying that penalty really stung. Bottom line, if you're stable, go yearly and save money; if you're unsure, pay a bit more for freedom. Just be honest with yourself about your plans!

title: Furnishing A First Apartment Cheaply
prompt_text: Your younger sister just moved into her very first apartment and it's completely empty. She's excited but doesn't have much money and feels overwhelmed about buying furniture and everything she needs. Your sister asks you how she should furnish the place without spending too much. What advice would you give?
template_hint: Reassure her, advise buying essentials first and going second-hand, give two reasons (saves money, easy to find good deals), add a personal example, and close warmly.
sample_response_band12: Aw, congratulations on your first place, that's so exciting! First things first, don't panic, you do not need to buy everything at once. My advice would be to focus on the essentials: a bed, somewhere to sit, and basic kitchen stuff. Everything else can wait. The big thing is to shop second-hand, honestly, you can save a fortune. Thrift stores, online marketplaces, and even family hand-me-downs are full of great furniture for a fraction of the price, and a lot of it is barely used. When I moved out, I got a perfectly good couch off a marketplace app for thirty dollars, and it lasted me years. Also, don't be shy about asking relatives if they have anything spare in storage, people are usually happy to help out a first-time renter. Trust me, mixing and matching cheaper pieces gives a place character anyway. In the end, your apartment will fill up over time, so enjoy the process and don't stress about making it perfect right away. You're going to love living on your own!

title: Downsizing To Save Money
prompt_text: Your friend is struggling with rising rent and is thinking about moving into a smaller, cheaper apartment to save money. They like their current spacious place but feel the cost is becoming too much to handle. Your friend asks you whether downsizing is a good idea. What advice would you give?
template_hint: Validate the worry, recommend downsizing, give two reasons (financial relief and the upside of less stuff), include a quick example, and close on reassurance.
sample_response_band12: To be honest, I think downsizing is a really smart move, so I'd definitely encourage it. I know you love your current place, but if the rent is stressing you out every month, that's a sign the space isn't worth the cost. For one thing, moving somewhere smaller and cheaper gives you real financial breathing room, and that extra money each month can go toward savings or just not feeling anxious when rent is due. What's more, smaller places force you to declutter, and honestly, most of us own way more stuff than we actually use. My sister downsized last year, and she told me she felt lighter and freer with less to clean and maintain. Just remember to measure your furniture before you move so you know what'll fit, because that part trips people up. And give the new neighbourhood a quick visit first to make sure you like it. All in all, peace of mind and a healthier budget are worth more than extra square footage. I really think you'll feel relieved once it's done!

title: Subletting A Room To A Stranger
prompt_text: Your classmate wants to rent out the spare room in their apartment to help cover the rent, but the only interested person is a complete stranger they found online. They like the extra income but feel uneasy about living with someone they don't know. Your classmate asks you whether they should go ahead with it. What advice would you give?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Acknowledge the unease, say it's fine to proceed but only after screening, give two reasons (income vs. safety) with concrete steps, add an example, and close supportively.
sample_response_band12: I get why you're hesitant, living with a stranger is a big deal, but the extra income could really help, so I think it's worth considering, just carefully. Before anything else, screen the person properly. Meet them in person first, maybe over coffee in public, and ask for references from a past landlord or roommate. That way you get a real sense of who they are instead of just relying on a few online messages. On top of that, I'd set clear ground rules from day one, things like cleaning, guests, and quiet hours, and ideally put them in a simple written agreement. It saves so many headaches later. A friend of mine sublet to someone she found online, and because she did a video call and checked references first, it actually turned into a great living situation. But here's the key: if something feels off when you meet them, trust your gut and walk away, no amount of rent money is worth feeling unsafe in your own home. At the end of the day, splitting the rent is great, just protect yourself first!

title: Roommate Who Never Cleans Up
prompt_text: Your friend just moved into a shared apartment, but one of their roommates never cleans up after themselves. There are dirty dishes in the sink for days and crumbs all over the kitchen counter. Your friend feels uncomfortable bringing it up and doesn't want to start a fight. What advice would you give your friend about handling this situation?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
At the end of the day
Another thing to keep in mind
template_hint: Open warmly acknowledging the frustration, give one clear piece of advice (have a calm direct chat), back it with two reasons, add a concrete example of what to say, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Hey, I totally get why this is bugging you, and honestly anyone would feel the same way. If I were you, the first thing I'd do is have a calm, friendly chat with your roommate instead of letting it build up. The reason I say that is people often don't even realize they're being messy, so a quick conversation can fix it before it turns into resentment. On top of that, talking early keeps things light, which really matters when you're sharing a small space. What I'd suggest is keeping it casual, like saying, "Hey, could we both try to wash our dishes right after dinner?" That way it sounds like a team thing, not an attack. For example, my cousin did exactly that and her roommate actually thanked her for being upfront. At the end of the day, being honest but kind is the way to go, and I'm sure once you talk it out, things will get a lot smoother. Good luck!

title: Roommate Late Paying The Bills
prompt_text: Your cousin shares an apartment with a roommate who is constantly late paying their share of the rent and utility bills. Your cousin keeps having to cover the difference and is starting to feel stressed about money. They asked you what they should do. What advice would you give your cousin?
template_hint: Open with empathy about the money stress, give one main solution (set up a clear payment system with a fixed date), support it with two reasons, give a concrete tool or example, then close reassuringly.
sample_response_band12: Oh, that sounds really stressful, and you definitely shouldn't have to keep paying out of pocket for someone else. Here's what I'd do. For starters, I'd sit down with your roommate and agree on a clear due date, maybe a few days before the bills are actually owed. To be honest, the main reason people pay late is that there's no real system, so setting a firm date usually solves half the problem right there. Beyond that, I'd suggest using a shared bill-splitting app, so it's not on you to chase them every month. For example, my friend started using one, and her roommate suddenly became way more reliable because everyone could see what was owed. If that doesn't work and they keep slipping, then it's fair to have a more serious talk. The bottom line is, you need to protect your own finances first. I'm sure once you put a system in place, this will sort itself out pretty quickly. Hang in there!

title: Dividing Household Chores Fairly
prompt_text: Your friend has just moved in with two new roommates and wants to figure out how to divide the household chores so everyone does their fair share. Your friend wants to set things up fairly from the start to avoid arguments later. They came to you for ideas. What advice would you give your friend?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Open by praising the proactive approach, give one core piece of advice (create a rotating chore schedule together), support with two reasons, give a concrete example of how to set it up, then close on a positive note.
sample_response_band12: That's such a smart move, sorting this out before any problems pop up. My advice would be to sit down with both roommates and make a chore chart together, where everyone has a say. What works really well is rotating the tasks every week, so no one gets stuck doing the worst job, like cleaning the bathroom, every single time. The reason I love this is that it feels fair, and when people feel things are fair, they're way more likely to actually do their part. On top of that, having it written down means there's no "I thought it was your turn" arguments later. For example, my roommates and I used a simple whiteboard on the fridge, and it honestly kept the peace for the whole year. Another tip is to check in once a month to see if anything needs tweaking. At the end of the day, the key is doing it as a team, not telling people what to do. You've got the right idea, so I think it'll go great!

title: Roommate's Partner Always Over
prompt_text: Your friend is frustrated because their roommate's boyfriend is at their apartment almost every single day and night. He uses the shared space and eats their food, and your friend feels like they have a third roommate who isn't paying rent. Your friend doesn't want to seem unfriendly, but they really need some privacy back. What advice would you give your friend?
connector_phrases: If I were in your shoes
When it comes down to it
template_hint: Open by validating the awkwardness, give one clear piece of advice (raise it gently as a boundaries-and-fairness issue, not a personal attack), support with two reasons, give a concrete script, then close with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Ugh, that's a tricky one, but you're absolutely allowed to want your own space back. If I were in your shoes, I'd bring it up gently, framing it as a boundaries thing rather than anything against the boyfriend personally. Honestly, the reason that matters is that if you attack him, your roommate will just get defensive, but if you talk about privacy and fairness, it's much easier to hear. The way I see it, there are really two issues, your privacy and the shared food, so I'd tackle both. What I'd recommend is saying something like, "I love hanging out, but lately I never get the place to myself. Could we agree on a couple of nights that are just us roommates?" Beyond that, you can kindly mention that since he eats here so often, maybe he could chip in for groceries. For example, my sister tried setting "guest nights" with her roommate and it worked perfectly. When it comes down to it, you deserve to feel comfortable in your own home, and I'm sure you'll handle it with grace.

title: Setting House Rules With Roommate
prompt_text: Your younger sister is about to move in with a roommate she doesn't know very well. She wants to agree on some house rules in advance, like quiet hours, having guests over, and sharing food, but she isn't sure how to start that conversation. She asked you for some guidance. What advice would you give your sister?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Open with encouragement for being proactive, give one main piece of advice (have an open conversation early and agree on rules together), support with two reasons, give concrete topics or an example, then close warmly.
sample_response_band12: Aw, I'm so glad you're thinking about this ahead of time, that's honestly the secret to a happy home. The first thing I'd do is set up a relaxed chat with your roommate in the first week, maybe over coffee, before any habits get set. That matters because it's way easier to agree on rules when nobody's annoyed yet, and it shows you both respect each other. What I'd suggest is bringing a little list to talk through, like quiet hours, having guests over, and whether you share food or keep it separate. It also helps to frame things as questions, like "How do you feel about late nights?" rather than handing over a rulebook. On top of that, be ready to compromise, because it goes both ways. For example, my roommate and I agreed on quiet hours after ten, and we never once argued about noise. At the end of the day, a quick honest talk now saves a ton of stress later. You're going to do great, sis!

title: Roommate Borrows Without Asking
prompt_text: Your classmate is upset because their roommate keeps borrowing their things, like clothes, kitchen items, and even their laptop charger, without ever asking first. Your classmate finds it disrespectful but worries that saying something will make the apartment feel tense. They want to know how to handle it. What advice would you give your classmate?
connector_phrases: When all is said and done
A good way to start is
template_hint: Open by validating the feeling of being disrespected, give one clear piece of advice (calmly ask the roommate to check first), support with two reasons, give a concrete phrasing example, then close with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Yeah, I'd be annoyed too, because it really does feel disrespectful when someone uses your stuff without checking. Here's what I think. If I were you, I'd say something soon, while it's still small, instead of letting it pile up into a big blow-up. The main reason is that your roommate probably thinks it's no big deal, so they have no idea it's bothering you. Besides that, speaking up early actually keeps things calm, because you can say it nicely instead of exploding later. A good way to start is keeping it light but clear, like, "Hey, I don't mind sharing, but could you give me a heads-up before borrowing my charger? Sometimes I need it." That way you're not banning them, just asking for a quick check-in. For example, my brother did this with his roommate and it fixed the problem overnight. When all is said and done, your things are yours, and it's totally fair to set that boundary. I think you'll handle it perfectly!

title: Choosing A Trustworthy New Roommate
prompt_text: Your friend's old roommate is moving out, so they need to find someone new to share their apartment. Your friend is nervous about picking the wrong person and ending up with someone unreliable or untrustworthy. They asked you how they should go about choosing a good roommate. What advice would you give your friend?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Open by acknowledging the worry, give one core piece of advice (meet candidates in person and ask the right questions), support with two reasons, give a concrete step like checking references, then close encouragingly.
sample_response_band12: I totally understand being nervous, picking the wrong roommate can make home life miserable, so it's worth being careful. What I'd recommend is, first and foremost, meeting a few people in person rather than just texting, because you really get a feel for someone face to face. The reason that matters is you can tell pretty quickly if your lifestyles match, like whether they're tidy, quiet, or up all night. On top of that, I'd ask practical questions, such as how they handle bills and whether they have guests over a lot, since those are the things that usually cause problems. Another thing worth doing is asking for a reference, maybe from a past roommate or landlord, just to confirm they're reliable. For example, my sister did a quick coffee chat and a reference check, and she found an amazing roommate that way. At the end of the day, trust your gut, because if something feels off, it usually is. Trust me when I say, taking your time now is so worth it.

title: Dealing With A Micromanaging Boss
prompt_text: Your coworker is feeling frustrated and stressed because their new manager checks every small task, asks for constant updates, and rewrites their emails before they are sent. Your coworker feels they are not trusted and is starting to dread going into the office. They have asked you how to handle the situation. What advice would you give your coworker about dealing with a micromanaging boss?
template_hint: Empathize with the frustration, recommend one main strategy (proactively over-communicate to earn trust), support it with two reasons, give a concrete tactic like a weekly summary email, then end with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Ugh, I totally get why you're stressed; nobody likes feeling watched over their shoulder all day. Here's what I'd do, though. First of all, try giving your boss updates before they even ask. Trust me on this, when managers micromanage, it's usually because they're anxious, not because they think you're incapable. So if you stay one step ahead, you actually take that worry off their plate. For example, you could send a short Friday email summarizing what you finished and what's coming next week. That way, they feel in the loop without hovering. Another thing I'd suggest is calmly setting some boundaries; maybe ask for a quick weekly check-in instead of constant interruptions. Rather than seeing it as a complaint, frame it as 'this would help me be more productive.' Give it a few weeks, and I bet they'll start backing off once they trust your work. Hang in there, you've got this, and remember it often says more about them than about you.

title: Requesting Permanent Work From Home
prompt_text: Your neighbour has been working from home a few days a week and now wants to ask their boss for a permanent full-time work-from-home arrangement. They believe they are more productive at home and would save a long daily commute, but they are nervous about how to bring it up and worried the boss might say no. They have asked for your advice. What would you tell your neighbour about making this request?
template_hint: Acknowledge the nerves, recommend framing the request around business benefits with evidence, give two reasons, suggest proposing a trial period as a concrete example, and close supportively.
sample_response_band12: I think that's a totally reasonable thing to ask for, so don't be too nervous about it. What I'd recommend is going in with a clear plan rather than just asking out of the blue. The key thing is to frame it around what helps the company, not only what helps you. For example, pull together some proof that you've been just as productive, or even more so, on your remote days; maybe the projects you finished or the deadlines you hit. Before anything else, your boss is going to wonder 'will the work still get done?', so answering that upfront makes it an easy yes. Another reason this works is that you can point to the time you save on commuting and put that energy back into your job. My advice would be to suggest a three-month trial instead of asking for it permanently right away. That way, it feels low-risk for your boss, and your track record does the convincing. Go for it; the worst they can say is 'not yet.' Good luck!

title: Unsure About a Big Promotion
prompt_text: A relative of yours has been offered a promotion to a management position at their company. They feel flattered, but they are afraid of the extra responsibility and worry about failing in front of their team. They keep thinking about turning it down and have come to you for advice. What would you tell them?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Validate the self-doubt, recommend accepting the promotion, give two reasons (the company already believes in them and leadership skills grow on the job), add a concrete example like asking for a mentor or management training, and end with confidence.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, the fact that you're nervous tells me you'll take this seriously, and that's exactly what good managers do. If you ask me, you should accept it. The thing is, nobody ever feels one hundred percent ready for a step up, so that self-doubt is completely normal and it doesn't mean you can't do the job. Keep in mind that your company chose you out of everyone, so they clearly see something in you that you might not see yet. More importantly, leadership is a skill you grow into, not something you're born knowing. For example, you could ask your boss for a mentor or some management training in the first few months, so you're not figuring it all out alone. When I took on more responsibility, I felt the same way, and within a few months it felt natural. At the end of the day, turning it down out of fear is a regret you don't want. Say yes, and grow into it!

title: Negotiating a Low Salary Offer
prompt_text: A friend of yours has been offered a new job they really want, but the salary is lower than they expected and less than what similar roles usually pay. They are tempted to just accept it so they don't lose the opportunity, but a part of them feels the offer is too low. They have asked whether they should try to negotiate. What advice would you give your friend?
template_hint: Reassure that negotiating is normal, recommend making a counteroffer backed by research, give two reasons, add a concrete tactic like citing market rates or negotiating non-salary perks, and close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: My honest advice? Don't just accept it; at least try to negotiate. First off, asking for more is completely normal, and a good employer won't pull the offer just because you negotiated politely. Here's the thing, if similar roles pay more, you have every reason to make a counteroffer. What I'd do is look up the market rate for that position in your city, then go back and say something like, 'I'm really excited about this role, and based on what comparable positions pay, I was hoping for X.' That shows you've done your homework, and it's hard to argue with facts. On top of salary, remember you can negotiate other things too, like extra vacation days, a signing bonus, or a salary review in six months. So even if they can't move much on pay, you still come out ahead. Whatever you do, don't sell yourself short out of fear; know your worth. Bottom line, the worst case is they say no and you still have the job. Go get what you deserve!

title: A Boss Who Plays Favourites
prompt_text: Your classmate has a part-time job where the manager clearly favours one or two employees, who get the best shifts, the easy tasks, and most of the praise. Meanwhile, your classmate feels overlooked no matter how hard they work, and they are starting to feel discouraged. They have asked you what to do about a boss who plays favourites. What advice would you give them?
template_hint: Acknowledge how unfair it feels, recommend staying professional while increasing visibility of their contributions, give two reasons, add a concrete example like a one-on-one with the boss, and end with perspective.
sample_response_band12: I hear you; that's genuinely frustrating, and it's hard to stay motivated when it feels like the deck is stacked against you. But here's what I'd focus on: making your good work impossible to ignore. For starters, try keeping a quick record of what you accomplish, like extra shifts you covered or problems you solved. That way, when review time comes, you have real examples instead of just a feeling. Beyond that, I'd ask your manager for a short one-on-one and calmly say you'd love more responsibility or better shifts, and ask what you can do to get there. A lot of the time, favourites just happen to be more visible, not more talented, so putting yourself on the radar really helps. Try not to badmouth the favoured coworkers, though; staying professional protects your reputation. In the long run, if nothing changes no matter what you do, it might be a sign this isn't the right place for you. But give it a fair shot first. You deserve to be seen!

title: Choosing Between Two Job Offers
prompt_text: A friend of yours has just received two job offers at the same time and feels completely torn about which one to accept. One pays more but comes with long hours and little flexibility, while the other pays a bit less but has a great team and more room to grow. They have to decide within a few days and don't know what to do. They have asked for your advice on what they should choose.
connector_phrases: That's a great problem to have
The way I'd look at it
When it comes down to it
template_hint: Acknowledge it's a good problem to have, recommend the option with growth and balance while respecting their priorities, give two reasons, suggest listing deal-breakers as a concrete method, and close by encouraging them to trust their gut.
sample_response_band12: First of all, that's a great problem to have, so congratulations! Personally, I'd lean toward the second offer, even though it pays a little less. The way I'd look at it, money matters, sure, but long hours with no flexibility can burn you out fast, and that affects everything else in your life. Most of all, the second job gives you room to grow and a team you actually enjoy, and that's worth a lot over the years. A higher salary is nice, but if you're miserable, you'll be job-hunting again in a year anyway. Here's a trick that might help, though: write down your top three deal-breakers, like growth, balance, and pay, and see which job ticks more boxes. For example, if work-life balance is what keeps you sane, the answer becomes pretty clear. When it comes down to it, also trust your gut; if one offer just feels right, that instinct usually knows something. You really can't lose here, so take a breath and pick the one that excites you. You've got this!

title: Owning Up To A Mistake
prompt_text: A close coworker comes to you looking really worried. They made a mistake on a big client order that cost the company a few thousand dollars, and so far nobody knows it was their fault. They are torn between quietly fixing what they can and hoping it blows over, or going to their manager and admitting exactly what happened. What advice would you give your coworker?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Open with reassurance, recommend coming clean to the manager, give two reasons (trust and earlier fixes are easier), add a quick example, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Okay, first of all, take a deep breath, because honestly everybody messes up at some point, so don't beat yourself up over this. If I were you, I'd go to your manager and own up to it as soon as possible. The main reason is that the truth almost always comes out eventually, and it looks so much worse if it seems like you were hiding it. On top of that, the sooner you flag it, the more time the team has to do damage control before things get even costlier. For example, my friend once double-billed a client by accident, but because she admitted it right away, her boss actually thanked her for being upfront and they sorted it out quietly. What I'd suggest is going in with a clear plan, like, here's what happened, here's how I'll fix it, and here's how I'll prevent it next time. That shows you're responsible, not careless. At the end of the day, your honesty will protect your credibility way more than staying silent. You've got this.

title: Responding To An Unfair Review
prompt_text: A good friend just got their yearly performance review at work, and they feel it was really unfair. Their manager gave them a low rating, criticized things they don't think are even part of their job, and ignored all the extra projects they took on. They're not sure whether to push back formally, talk it out calmly, or just let it go. What advice would you give your friend?
template_hint: Validate the frustration, advise a calm follow-up meeting backed by evidence, give two reasons (clarity and a paper trail), include an example, then close warmly.
sample_response_band12: I totally get why you're upset, that does sound really unfair, especially after all the extra work you put in. But here's what I'd do: instead of firing off an angry email, take a day to cool down and then ask your manager for a calm follow-up chat. First off, when you stay composed, people actually listen, and it's harder for them to dismiss you as being defensive. The thing is, you want to come in with proof, so pull together a list of those extra projects and any positive feedback you've gotten. Another reason to do this in person is that a lot of misunderstandings can get cleared up just by talking it through. Say, for instance, your manager simply didn't realize how much you'd taken on, this gives them a chance to correct the record. If the meeting doesn't help, then you can look into a formal rebuttal through HR and document everything. All in all, push back, but do it smartly and professionally. You deserve to be heard.

title: Quitting Without A Backup Job
prompt_text: Your cousin is completely burned out at their current job and is seriously thinking about handing in their resignation, even though they don't have another job lined up yet. They feel they need a clean break to recover and job-hunt properly, but they're worried about money and dreading another month in that office. They want to know whether you think quitting first is a smart move. What advice would you give your cousin?
template_hint: Acknowledge the burnout, advise caution by checking savings first, give two reasons (financial safety and job-search leverage), add a what-if example, then close supportively.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, I really feel for you, burnout is no joke, and I can hear how exhausted you are. My honest take is, before you hand in your notice, take a hard look at your savings and figure out how many months you could realistically cover. One big reason is that job hunting can take way longer than people expect, and running out of money would only add to the stress you're already under. That said, if you've got a solid financial cushion, even a short break could help you recharge and interview with way more confidence. Just imagine walking into interviews calm and rested instead of drained, that comes across, and employers notice it. So what I'd suggest is lining up a few leads or negotiating some vacation first, so you've got something to fall back on before you leave. But if it's genuinely affecting your health, your wellbeing has to come first. Bottom line, if you can take this leap safely, do it, and I'm fully rooting for you.

title: First-Day Nerves At A New Job
prompt_text: A friend of yours is starting a brand-new job tomorrow morning and they are really nervous. They keep worrying that they'll forget people's names, ask silly questions, or make a bad first impression on their new team. They called you tonight to calm down and asked how they should handle their first day. What advice would you give your friend?
connector_phrases: If I were in your shoes
template_hint: Reassure them the nerves are normal, advise arriving early and asking questions, give two reasons (good impression and faster learning), add a personal example, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Aw, don't worry, those nerves are completely normal, it just means you care about doing well. If I were in your shoes, I'd get a good night's sleep and show up about ten minutes early tomorrow. The main reason is that arriving relaxed and on time makes a great first impression, and it gives you a minute to settle in before things get busy. Plus, here's a little secret, nobody expects you to know everything on day one, so please ask questions instead of pretending. Trust me, asking is how you learn fastest, and it actually makes you look engaged, not clueless. Like when I started my last job, I forgot half the names too, so I kept a small notebook to jot them down, and that little trick really saved me. Most importantly, just be friendly and approachable, smile, say hi, and let yourself ease into it. Go easy on yourself, okay? You're going to do great, honestly.

title: Whether To Repeat Office Gossip
prompt_text: A coworker you're friendly with accidentally overheard two managers talking about possible layoffs in another department. They're not sure whether to keep it quiet or warn a friend who works there, since that friend might want time to prepare. They feel torn between being a good friend and staying out of office drama. They turn to you and ask what you would do in their situation. What advice would you give your coworker?
connector_phrases: When it comes down to it
template_hint: Caution against spreading unconfirmed news, advise keeping it private, give two reasons (it's unverified and could backfire), add a what-if example, then close with a balanced takeaway.
sample_response_band12: Ooh, I'd be careful here, because this is genuinely tricky. The way I see it, I'd keep it to yourself for now and not pass it along. For one thing, this is really just hearsay at this point. You overheard part of a conversation, and you don't actually know if those layoffs are confirmed or just being kicked around. If you warn your friend and it turns out to be nothing, you'd cause them a ton of stress for no reason. Besides, this could seriously backfire on you. If word gets out and it's traced back to you, your managers might see you as someone who can't be trusted with sensitive information. Picture this: your friend panics, mentions it to someone, and suddenly the whole office knows. That's a real mess. What I'd suggest instead is staying out of the drama and just being a supportive friend in general. When it comes down to it, protecting your own reputation and not spreading rumours is the safer call here. A little discretion really pays off in the long run.

title: Returning to Work After Absence
prompt_text: Your neighbour is about to go back to work after being away for almost a year for health reasons. They are excited but also anxious because a lot has changed, including new coworkers and new systems. They worry that people will treat them differently or doubt their abilities. They asked you how they should approach their return. What advice would you give your neighbour?
template_hint: Acknowledge it's a big step, advise easing back in and asking for a catch-up, give two reasons (avoiding overwhelm and rebuilding confidence), add an example, then close warmly.
sample_response_band12: First things first, congratulations, going back after a whole year is a big deal, and it's completely normal to feel a bit nervous about it. What I'd really suggest is easing back in slowly rather than trying to prove yourself all at once. The reason being, you've been out of the loop for a while, so if you pace yourself, you'll avoid burning out in the first week. And another thing, ask your manager for a quick catch-up session to get up to speed on the new systems and meet the new faces. That way you won't feel lost, and it shows initiative. Take, for example, my aunt, when she came back from her leave, she set up short chats with a few teammates, and within two weeks she was right back in the swing of things. Honestly, don't be afraid to lean on people and ask questions, nobody expects you to know everything overnight. In the end, just go easy on yourself and trust that your skills are still there. You'll find your rhythm again before you know it.

title: Requesting Time Off Tactfully
prompt_text: A classmate of yours works part-time and needs to ask their boss for several days off to deal with an important personal matter. They are nervous because it is a busy season at work, and they do not want to share too many private details or seem unreasonable. Your classmate asks how they should go about requesting the time off. What advice would you give your classmate?
template_hint: Reassure them it's a reasonable request, advise asking early and privately, give two reasons (more notice helps and brevity protects privacy), add an example, then close encouragingly.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, don't stress too much, everybody needs time off for personal stuff now and then, so this is a totally reasonable thing to ask. Here's the thing, if I were you, I'd ask as early as possible and do it in a private moment, not in front of the whole team. Mainly because the more advance warning you give, the easier it is for your boss to cover your shifts, even during a busy season. What's more, you don't owe anyone the full story, so keep it brief, just say it's an important personal matter you need to take care of, and that you'll make sure your work is handled. Let's say you offer to swap shifts with a coworker or finish things up before you go, that shows you're being responsible, and bosses really appreciate that. So all in all, be upfront, give plenty of notice, and stay professional about it. I'm sure it'll go smoother than you think, so try not to worry.

title: Choosing Between Two Universities
prompt_text: Your younger cousin has been accepted to two universities and can't decide which offer to accept. One is a large, well-known school far from home, and the other is a smaller school in your cousin's hometown. She knows you went through the same thing a few years ago, so she has come to you for help. What advice would you give her about how to choose between the two universities?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Open warmly to acknowledge the dilemma, give one clear recommendation, back it with two reasons (program fit and finances/distance), add a short personal example, then close with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Hey, I totally get why you're torn, because honestly I lost sleep over this exact same decision. Okay, here's what I'd suggest: don't just chase the big name. The main thing is to look at the actual program you want to study and ask which school does it better. If the smaller school in town has stronger professors in your field, that matters way more than prestige. On top of that, think about money and distance. Living at home could save you thousands a year, and that's huge when you graduate debt-free. When I picked my school, I went for the flashy one and ended up homesick and broke, so trust me on this. What I'd really do is visit both campuses if you can, sit in on a class, and just notice where you feel like you belong. Your gut tells you a lot. At the end of the day, there's no wrong choice here, so don't stress too much. Whatever you pick, you'll make it work, and I'm always here to talk it through. Good luck!

title: Studying Abroad For A Year
prompt_text: A close friend has been offered the chance to spend a full year studying abroad in another country. She is excited, but she keeps going back and forth because she is nervous about leaving her family, friends, and part-time job behind. She trusts your opinion and asks you what she should do. What advice would you give her?
connector_phrases: When it comes down to it
template_hint: Acknowledge her excitement and nerves, recommend she go for it, give two reasons (rare opportunity and personal growth), offer a practical tip to ease the worry, and end with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Okay, deep breath. Honestly, if you ask me, this is a once-in-a-lifetime thing and you should absolutely go for it. The way I see it, a year abroad will broaden your horizons in a way nothing else can. You'll experience a whole new culture, meet people from all over, and come back more confident and independent. Plus, opportunities like this almost never come around twice, especially once you finish school and start working full time. More importantly, I know you, and I really think you'd regret saying no far more than you'd regret saying yes. As for the family and friends part, that's real, but a year flies by, and with video calls these days you'll barely feel the distance. My sister did a year overseas and she still says it was the best decision she ever made, even though she cried at the airport. The job will still be there, or honestly you'll find a better one. When it comes down to it, don't let a little fear make this choice for you. Go for it. I'm really excited for you.

title: Whether To Pursue A Master's
prompt_text: A coworker who finished her bachelor's degree a few years ago is thinking about going back to school for a master's degree. She is worried about the cost, the time commitment, and whether it will actually help her career. She knows you've thought carefully about further education, so she asks you what she should do. What advice would you give her?
connector_phrases: What I'd really focus on is
template_hint: Validate her concerns, give a conditional recommendation tied to her career goals, support it with two reasons (career payoff and timing/cost), share a concrete example, and close supportively.
sample_response_band12: Hey Sandra, that's a big one, but I'm glad you asked. Here's the thing, a master's isn't automatically worth it. It really depends on your field and your goals. What I'd really focus on is whether the degree actually opens doors in your industry. For one thing, in some careers a master's leads straight to promotions and higher pay, so the return on investment is clear. Then again, in other fields experience matters more than another diploma, and you'd lose money and years for not much gain. So my honest advice is to talk to a few people who already have the job you want, and ask whether their master's actually mattered. My cousin did exactly that before committing, and she found out a short certificate would get her the same raise for a fraction of the cost. Also, think hard about the time, because studying while working full time is exhausting. Bottom line, if it lines up with your long-term goals, go for it. If it were me, I'd just do that research first. Whatever you decide, I'm rooting for you.

title: Transferring To A Different School
prompt_text: Your neighbour's daughter is partway through her degree but is unhappy at her current college. She is thinking about transferring to a different school, but she's worried about losing credits and starting over socially. The family knows you understand the school system, so they ask you to talk to her. What advice would you give her about transferring to a different school?
connector_phrases: The reason I say that is
template_hint: Show empathy, recommend she investigate before deciding, give two reasons (protecting credits and confirming the new school fixes the real problem), add a practical step, and reassure her.
sample_response_band12: Hi Emma, I heard you're not loving things at your college, and I'm sorry, that's tough. First things first, my advice would be don't make any sudden moves until you've done a bit of homework. The reason I say that is transferring can cost you credits, and you don't want to lose a whole year of work. So before anything, book a meeting with an academic advisor at the new school and ask exactly which credits will carry over. Besides, I'd gently ask yourself what's really making you unhappy. If it's the program or the location, then a fresh start makes total sense. But if it's mostly feeling like you don't fit in yet, sometimes that just takes a semester to settle in. A friend of mine almost transferred in her second year, gave it one more term, found her people, and ended up loving it. That said, if your gut says this place isn't right, trust that, because being happy matters for your grades too. In the long run, you want a school that feels like home. Take your time, and call me anytime, okay?

title: Taking a Gap Year
prompt_text: Your younger brother just finished high school but feels burned out and unsure about what to study. He is thinking about taking a gap year before college to travel and work, but your parents are worried he won't go back. He wants to know what you really think. What advice would you give him about taking a gap year before college?
template_hint: Acknowledge the burnout, support the gap year with a condition (a clear plan), give two reasons (recovery and direction), add a concrete suggestion, and close warmly.
sample_response_band12: Look, little bro, I actually think a gap year could be a great idea, so don't let Mom and Dad scare you off it. Honestly, you've been grinding through school for years and you sound completely burned out, so taking time to recharge makes total sense. The key, though, is that you can't just sit on the couch for twelve months. What I'd do is make a real plan: work and save money for a few months, then travel or volunteer. That way you get real-world experience and figure out what actually interests you, which helps you pick a major instead of guessing. Also, here's a tip that'll calm the parents down. Apply to college now and defer your spot for a year, so you've got a guaranteed place waiting and you won't lose momentum or talk yourself out of going back. My buddy did exactly that, worked at a ski resort, came back totally focused, and crushed first year. Just remember to make it count. I really think you'll come back a new person, so go for it.

title: Advise A Friend On School
prompt_text: A friend who works full time wants to go back to school but can't decide between an online program he can do from home and a traditional in-person program at a local college. He's worried about staying motivated if he studies online, but he's also worried about fitting in-person classes around his job. He really values your judgment and wants to know what you think he should do. What advice would you give him?
template_hint: Give one clear recommendation up front based on his work schedule and how he learns, back it with two reasons (for example flexibility around his job and staying accountable or motivated), add a short practical example or first step he can take, and close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Okay so, Daniel, I can tell you're really torn, but honestly, I think you should go with the online program. Here's what I'd do. The main reason is flexibility. You're working full time, so an online program means no commute and no schedule conflicts with your job. You can study early in the morning or late at night, whenever things calm down. On top of that, you said you're worried about staying motivated, and I get that, but you can fix that with a routine. Just block out the same two hours every evening and tell me your goals so I can help keep you accountable. My cousin did her whole certificate online while working, and the trick was treating each lesson like a real appointment she couldn't skip. If I were you, I'd start with just one online course this term as a test run, see how the self-discipline feels, and then commit to the full program once you know it works. Either way, I think it's awesome that you're going back to school, and I'm totally here to support you through it.

title: Choosing Between Two Scholarships
prompt_text: Your niece has been offered two scholarships and has to choose one. The first is a larger award at a school she likes a little less, while the second is a smaller award at her dream school, which means she would still have to cover some costs herself. She feels overwhelmed and asks you to help her decide. What advice would you give her?
template_hint: Calm her down, recommend she do the math first, give two reasons (real cost difference and long-term happiness/fit), add a concrete example, then close encouragingly.
sample_response_band12: First of all, take a breath, sweetheart. Honestly, both of these are wonderful problems to have, so be proud of yourself. Here's what I think. Before you let your heart decide, sit down and actually do the math. For starters, work out the real out-of-pocket costs at your dream school after the smaller scholarship. If the gap is small and your family can manage it, then going where you'll be happiest is totally worth it, because loving your school helps you do your best work. But then again, if the bigger award is basically a full ride and the difference is thousands of dollars a year, that financial pressure can weigh on you for years after graduation. My friend chose her dream school with loans, and she's still paying them off ten years later. So look at the bigger picture, not just this fall. All things considered, my two cents is to pick the option that lets you finish without drowning in debt, as long as you can still be happy there. Whatever you choose, you're going to shine.

title: Teammate Not Pulling Their Weight
prompt_text: A classmate of yours is working on a graded group project, but one teammate keeps missing meetings and never finishes their share of the work. The deadline is close, and your classmate feels like they are carrying the whole team. They are not sure whether to confront the teammate, cover the work themselves, or tell the professor, so they have asked you what they should do. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Validate the frustration, advise a calm direct conversation with the teammate first, then suggest documenting and involving the professor only as a backup, support both with reasons, add a quick example, and close supportively.
sample_response_band12: Okay, first of all, I'd be annoyed too, so that reaction is totally fair, but try not to just silently carry the whole thing. If I were you, the first thing I'd do is message your teammate privately and give them one specific task with a clear deadline, like, hey, can you have the slides done by Thursday? A lot of people slack off just because nobody's actually pinned them down, so being direct often fixes it right away. On top of that, I'd keep a quiet record of who did what in your group chat, because if it falls apart, you've got proof, and telling the professor isn't tattling, it's just honest. For example, my buddy did exactly this last term, sent one firm message, and suddenly his teammate started showing up. Just remember, don't redo their part for them, because that only trains them to keep coasting. At the end of the day, you deserve credit for your own work, so speak up early. You've totally got this.

title: Nervous About A Class Presentation
prompt_text: A close friend of yours has to give a presentation in front of their whole class next week, and they are terrified of speaking in public. They tell you they get shaky, forget their words, and are scared everyone will judge them. They have asked you for some advice on how to get through it. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd say is
template_hint: Reassure them the fear is normal, give one tip about rehearsing out loud and one about managing nerves in the moment, back each with a reason, add a quick personal example, and close with a confidence boost.
sample_response_band12: Aw, honestly, almost everyone gets nervous about this, so trust me, you are completely normal and you're going to be fine. The first thing I'd say is practice out loud, like really out loud, not just reading it in your head. Stand up, say it to your mirror or to me, three or four times, because once the words are in your mouth and not just your eyes, your brain stops freezing up.
