On top of that, make yourself a few cue cards with just keywords, not full sentences, so you've got a safety net if you blank. What really helps in the moment is taking one slow, deep breath before you start, and finding two or three friendly faces in the room to look at instead of the whole crowd. When I did that for my first big presentation, my hands stopped shaking within a minute. Honestly, nobody is judging you nearly as hard as you think, they're all just glad it's not their turn. You've got this, you'll be great.

title: Overwhelmed by a Long Thesis
prompt_text: Your cousin has just started a year-long thesis project and feels completely overwhelmed by how big and open-ended it is. They keep putting off starting because they do not know where to begin, and the final deadline feels so far away that nothing feels urgent yet. They have asked you how to manage such a huge long-term project. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Acknowledge how daunting it feels, advise breaking the project into milestones with mini-deadlines and checking in regularly with a supervisor, justify each with a reason, add a concrete example, and close with grounded encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, a whole year on one project would overwhelm anyone, so don't beat yourself up for freezing. Here's what I'd do. For starters, stop thinking of it as one giant thesis and break it into small chunks with their own little deadlines, like finish the reading by next month, draft chapter one by the one after. The reason is, your brain can actually picture finishing one small piece, but it panics at the whole mountain, so milestones make it feel doable. The other big thing is, set up regular check-ins with your supervisor, even monthly, because a real deadline with another person waiting on you kills the procrastination instantly. For example, my friend wrote his entire thesis just by promising his advisor a few pages every two weeks, and he was never actually stressed. Believe me, the worst trap is telling yourself the deadline is far away, because suddenly it's a month out and nothing's done. At the end of the day, you don't have to do it all today, you just have to chip away at it. Start with one tiny step tomorrow.

title: Learning A New Language
prompt_text: A friend of yours wants to start learning a new language completely from scratch, but they feel lost about how to begin and worry they are too old to ever become fluent. They keep downloading apps and then giving up after a few days. They have asked you for advice on how to actually learn it and stick with it. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Reassure them they are not too old, give one tip on small daily practice and one on real-life immersion or speaking, back each with a reason, add a relatable example, and close by motivating them to start.
sample_response_band12: Hey, first off, you are absolutely not too old, people learn languages at every age, so let go of that worry right now. My biggest tip is to keep it small but daily, like just fifteen minutes every single morning. If I were you, I'd attach it to something you already do, like practising while your coffee brews, because tiny daily habits stick far better than cramming for hours and then quitting. Another thing that helps is using the language for real as soon as possible, so change your phone to that language, watch shows with subtitles, and say simple everyday phrases out loud. Trust me on this, you learn way faster by actually speaking and making mistakes than by silently doing app drills. Plus, those embarrassing little errors are exactly what make the words stick. When I started learning French, I felt hopeless for weeks, but once I forced myself to chat with a coworker, it just clicked. At the end of the day, slow and steady wins this one. Start today, be patient, and you'll surprise yourself.

title: Retaking A Failed Class
prompt_text: Your younger sibling failed a course last semester and now has to retake the whole thing. They feel embarrassed and discouraged, and they are afraid the same thing will happen again. They are not sure how to approach the class differently this time. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: What I'd really focus on is
template_hint: Reassure them and remove the shame first, advise figuring out what went wrong last time and getting help early this time, support each with a reason, add an encouraging example, and close warmly.
sample_response_band12: Listen, first of all, failing a class does not make you a failure. Tons of great students have retaken something, so please stop being so hard on yourself. Here's the thing, you actually have a big advantage this time because you've already seen the material once. What I'd really focus on is figuring out honestly what went wrong before. Was it that you fell behind early, didn't ask questions, or just ran out of time? Once you know the real reason, you can fix that exact thing instead of repeating it. On top of that, I'd get help the moment something feels confusing, so go to office hours, email the professor, form a study group, whatever it takes. The reason is, last time the gaps probably snowballed quietly, so catching them early changes everything. Trust me, my roommate retook chemistry, went to every office hour, and ended up acing it. Just remember, this is a fresh start, not a punishment. Go easy on yourself, show up consistently, and you'll get through it.

title: Fixing Last-Minute Study Habits
prompt_text: A classmate of yours admits they always leave studying until the very last night before a test and end up cramming everything in a panic. They know this habit is hurting their grades and their sleep, but they cannot seem to break it. They have asked you how they can build better study habits. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Acknowledge the habit is common, advise spreading study into short spaced sessions and removing distractions, back each with a reason, add a concrete example, and close encouragingly.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, almost everyone has done the panic-cram thing, so don't feel hopeless, you just need a better system. If you ask me, the trick is to stop studying for hours and instead do short, regular sessions, like thirty minutes a day spread across the week. The reason is, your brain remembers way more when you space it out, whereas cramming one night just dumps everything and most of it leaks out by morning. Beyond that, I'd keep those sessions distraction-free, so put your phone in another room and pick one quiet spot. Believe me, twenty focused minutes beats two hours of scrolling between flashcards. What really helps is starting tiny, like opening your notes for five minutes, because once you start, momentum carries you. For example, I used to cram too, but the term I switched to little daily reviews, my grades jumped and I actually slept the night before exams. At the end of the day, small and steady beats one frantic night. You can totally kick this habit.

title: Afraid To Ask A Professor
prompt_text: A friend of yours is falling behind in one of their courses but is too intimidated to ask the professor for help. They worry the professor will think they are not smart enough or that their question is too basic. They have asked you how they can work up the courage to reach out. What advice would you give them?
vocabulary_tips: there's no such thing as a dumb question
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
template_hint: Reassure them the fear is normal and professors want to help, advise preparing one specific question and using email or office hours to start small, back each with a reason, add an example, and close with warm encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Trust me, I know that feeling, but here's something that helps: professors genuinely want students to come to them, it's literally part of their job, so you're not bothering anyone. The first thing I'd do is take the pressure off by sending a short, friendly email instead of walking in cold. Just say, hi, I'm a bit stuck on this topic, could I drop by your office hours? Starting in writing is way less scary, and it breaks the ice. Plus, what works really well is coming prepared with one specific question rather than a vague I don't get it. The reason is, a focused question shows you've actually tried, and honestly, that impresses professors way more than staying silent. For example, my classmate was terrified to ask anything, but once she emailed and went in with two clear questions, the professor was so warm that she went back every week. Just remember, there's no such thing as a dumb question. Reach out this week, and you'll be so glad you did.

title: Sharing Care For An Aging Parent
prompt_text: A close friend of yours is struggling because her father is getting older and needs more help at home with cooking, taking his medication, and getting to appointments. She feels completely overwhelmed trying to handle everything on her own while still working full-time. She isn't sure whether to hire some outside help, ask other family members to share the load, or look into local senior services. What advice would you give her?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Open warmly with empathy, give one main piece of advice (share or outsource care), back it with two reasons, add a short concrete example, then close with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Oh, I can tell you're really stretched thin right now, and that's completely understandable. Honestly, the first thing I'd do is stop trying to carry all of this on your own. What I'd suggest is sitting down with your brothers and sisters and splitting up the responsibilities, because nobody can work full-time and be a full-time caregiver without burning out. On top of that, I'd look into the senior services in your area. A lot of cities have home support workers who can help with meals or drive your dad to his appointments for a really reasonable cost. For example, my aunt hired someone just two mornings a week, and it gave her a chance to breathe and actually enjoy her time with her mom again. At the end of the day, taking care of yourself isn't selfish, it's what keeps you able to take care of him. You don't have to do this alone, so please lean on the people and resources around you, okay?

title: Coping With an Overbearing Parent
prompt_text: Your cousin keeps getting unsolicited advice from his mother about how to raise his kids, run his household, and even how to spend his weekends. He loves her but feels she doesn't respect that he's a grown adult. He's frustrated and asks you how to handle it without starting a big fight. What advice would you give him?
connector_phrases: If I were in your shoes
template_hint: Acknowledge the tricky emotions, recommend a calm boundary-setting conversation, support it with two reasons, give a quick example phrase he could use, then close encouragingly.
sample_response_band12: I totally get why you're frustrated, that kind of thing wears you down even when it comes from love. Here's what I'd do: have a calm, private chat with your mom and gently let her know how you feel. The way I see it, you should lead with appreciation, something like, Mom, I really value your experience, but I need you to trust that I've got this. That works because it sets a boundary without making her feel attacked, and it shows her you're coming from respect, not anger. Another thing, try to pick your battles. You don't have to push back on every little comment, just the ones that really matter to you. For example, when my dad kept criticizing how I parent, I simply thanked him and changed the subject, and over time he eased off. Just remember, you're not being a bad son by asking for some space, you're protecting your peace. Bottom line, stay kind but firm, and she'll come around.

title: A Relative Who Always Asks Favours
prompt_text: Your neighbour has a relative who constantly asks her for favours, like borrowing money, getting rides, and watching his kids at the last minute. She finds it hard to say no because they are family, but she is starting to feel taken advantage of. She wants to set some limits without damaging the relationship. What advice would you give her?
template_hint: Validate her feelings, advise learning to say no with kind firmness, give two reasons, illustrate with a polite refusal example, then reassure her the relationship will survive.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, if I were you, I'd start by reminding myself that saying no doesn't make you a bad relative. It's clear you've got a generous heart, but you're feeling taken advantage of, and that resentment will only grow if you don't address it. What works for me is being firm but kind. You can still love someone and have limits. I'd start by saying yes only to things you genuinely have time for, and offering a polite no for the rest, like, I'd love to help, but I can't this time. That works for two reasons: it stops the resentment from building, and it actually teaches him to plan ahead instead of relying on you last minute. For example, a friend of mine started saying she was busy on weekends, and her brother quickly figured out other arrangements. Keep in mind, real family respects your time. All in all, set those limits gently, and you'll protect both your peace and the relationship.

title: Setting Boundaries With Difficult In-Laws
prompt_text: Your close friend's in-laws often drop by unannounced, comment on how she keeps her home, and expect to be involved in every decision she and her partner make. She wants a good relationship with them but feels she has no privacy or independence. She asks you how to set some boundaries without causing tension in the family. What advice would you give her?
template_hint: Empathize, recommend that she and her partner set boundaries together as a team, give two reasons, add a concrete example such as agreeing to call first, then close on a hopeful note.
sample_response_band12: I really feel for you, in-law relationships can be such a delicate balance. My advice would be to handle this as a team with your partner first. First and foremost, your partner should be the one to raise these things with their own parents, because boundaries always land better coming from their own child than from you. What's more, presenting a united front shows them this is a decision you both made together, not just you pushing them away. The key thing is to keep it warm and specific. For example, you could agree on something simple, like asking them to call before they come over, so you get a bit of a heads-up. If it were me, I'd frame it positively, something like, We love seeing you, and a quick call helps us make sure we're home and ready. That keeps the focus on welcoming them, not shutting them out. In the end, good boundaries actually make relationships healthier, so I really think this will bring you closer once the dust settles.

title: A Family Disagreement About Money
prompt_text: Your sister and her siblings are arguing over how to split the cost of caring for their elderly mother and other shared family expenses. Everyone has a different idea about who should pay what, and the disagreements are starting to hurt their relationships. She comes to you and asks how to handle the money conflict fairly before it gets any worse. What advice would you give her?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Show understanding, advise a calm family meeting with transparent numbers, give two reasons, suggest putting the agreement in writing as an example, then close by emphasizing the relationship over the money. Address the sister directly in the second person throughout.
sample_response_band12: Money fights within a family are honestly some of the hardest, so I really feel for you and your siblings. What I'd recommend is calling a calm family meeting where everyone sits down together with the actual numbers in front of them. To begin with, get everything out in the open, the real costs and what each person can realistically afford, because most of these arguments come from assumptions rather than facts. On top of that, fairness doesn't always mean an equal split. Someone earning more might contribute more in cash, while someone with less money might give time or hands-on care instead. The important thing is that everyone feels heard. For example, once you all agree on something, write it down so there's no confusion or resentment later. If I were you, I'd also ask a neutral relative to sit in and keep things calm if tempers flare. At the end of the day, your bond with your siblings is worth far more than any dollar amount, so go in with kindness and a willingness to compromise.

title: Teaching A Parent To Use Technology
prompt_text: Your friend is trying to teach his mother how to use her new smartphone so she can video call the family and send messages. The problem is she gets confused and frustrated every time, and now he's losing patience and worried he might give up. He asks you for advice on how to teach her more effectively. What advice would you give him?
template_hint: Reassure him this is normal, advise teaching slowly one step at a time with patience, give two reasons, add an example like a simple cheat sheet, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Oh, this is so common, don't be too hard on yourself or her. Here's the thing: the secret is to slow right down and teach just one thing at a time. What I'd do is start with only the basics she actually cares about, like answering a video call from the grandkids, and master that before moving on to anything else. That works because she won't feel overwhelmed, and small wins will build her confidence. Also, try to be really patient and never make her feel silly for asking the same question twice. Trust me on this, frustration is the fastest way to make someone shut down and give up. For example, I made my dad a simple cheat sheet with big pictures and arrows showing each step, and he loved being able to check it himself. Whatever you do, celebrate every little success, even just sending one emoji. Above all, remember she's learning something brand new, so a little encouragement goes a long way. She'll get there, I promise.

title: Feeling Guilty About Not Visiting Family
prompt_text: Your coworker has been so busy with work and her own kids that she rarely visits her parents, who live a few hours away. She feels guilty and worries they think she doesn't care, but she just can't seem to find the time. She asks you for advice on how to handle the situation and ease her guilt. What advice would you give her?
template_hint: Reassure her the guilt shows she cares, advise small consistent connection plus planned visits, give two reasons, add an example like a weekly call, then close by easing her guilt.
sample_response_band12: First of all, the fact that you feel guilty just shows how much you love them, so please give yourself some credit. If I were you, I wouldn't focus only on big visits, I'd focus on staying connected in small, regular ways. One simple thing you could do is set up a short weekly video call, maybe Sunday evenings, so it becomes a habit nobody has to remember. That helps for two reasons: your parents feel close to you even from a distance, and consistent little moments often mean more than one rushed visit a year. Plus, when you do go see them, try to make it real quality time without your phone, even if it's only once every couple of months. For example, a friend of mine started mailing little photos of the kids, and her mom absolutely treasured them. Try not to forget that your parents understand life gets busy, they raised you, after all. In the end, be kind to yourself, because a little effort done consistently truly goes a long way.

title: Friend Who Won't Repay Money
prompt_text: Your close friend lent a fairly large sum of money to a mutual acquaintance a few months ago to help with an emergency. The acquaintance promised to repay it within a couple of weeks, but months have passed and they keep avoiding the topic. Your friend feels uncomfortable and unsure how to bring it up without damaging the relationship. What advice would you give your friend about how to handle this situation?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Open warmly acknowledging how awkward this is for your friend, give one clear piece of advice (raise it directly and calmly), back it with two reasons, add a short example of how to phrase it, then close with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Okay, I totally get why this feels so awkward, but honestly, you can't keep letting it slide. If I were you, the first thing I'd do is bring it up directly but gently, maybe over coffee rather than by text. You could say something like, "Hey, I know things have been tight, but I really need that money back, so can we work out a plan?" There are two big reasons for this. First, the longer you stay silent, the more resentment builds, and that's what actually ruins relationships, not the money itself. Second, they might genuinely be embarrassed and waiting for you to make the first move. What I'd suggest is offering a small payment plan, like, "Even fifty dollars a month is fine." That way it feels doable for them and you still get repaid. At the end of the day, a decent person won't disappear over an honest conversation. So take a deep breath, be kind but clear, and I promise you'll feel so much lighter once it's out in the open.

title: Friend Who Cancels Plans Last Minute
prompt_text: You have a friend you really enjoy spending time with, but lately they keep cancelling your plans at the very last minute, sometimes when you are already on your way. It is starting to frustrate you, and you are not sure whether to say something or just quietly stop making plans with them. What advice would you give about handling this?
template_hint: Start by validating the frustration, give the main advice (talk to them honestly without accusing), support it with two reasons, add a quick example sentence to use, and close with an encouraging note.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, I'd be frustrated too, so your feelings make total sense here. The way I see it, you shouldn't just quietly give up on the friendship, because you clearly still value them. What I'd do is have a calm, honest chat and use "I" statements instead of blaming. Something like, "I really look forward to seeing you, so it stings when plans fall through last minute." There are two reasons this works better than staying silent. First, your friend may have no idea how much it's bothering you, so giving them a chance to explain is only fair. Plus, talking it out usually clears up whether they're just overwhelmed with life or genuinely being careless. On the other hand, if it keeps happening after you've spoken up, then it's okay to invest your energy elsewhere. In the end, good communication either fixes the problem or shows you where you stand. So speak up gently, and give them one real chance to step up.

title: Apologizing After A Serious Argument
prompt_text: You recently had a serious argument with a close friend, and things were said on both sides that you now regret. A few days have passed, you have both gone quiet, and you really want to repair the friendship, but you are nervous about reaching out first and worried about making things worse. You are not sure what to say or how to begin. What advice would you give about how to apologize and fix this?
connector_phrases: If I were in your shoes
When all is said and done
template_hint: Acknowledge the courage it takes, advise reaching out first with a genuine apology, give two reasons (pride versus the friendship, and timing), include an example opening line, and close with reassurance about being the bigger person.
sample_response_band12: First of all, the fact that you want to fix this already tells me how much this friendship means to you. If I were in your shoes, I'd reach out first rather than waiting for them to crack. Send a short, warm message like, "I've been thinking about our fight, and I'm really sorry for the things I said. Can we talk?" The key thing is to own your part without listing everything they did wrong, because the moment you start defending yourself, it stops being an apology. There are two reasons I'd act now. First, the longer you both stay silent, the more the distance feels permanent, and pride is a terrible reason to lose a good friend. More importantly, a sincere apology almost always lowers the other person's guard, so they're far more likely to apologize back. Beyond that, do it in person or by call if you can, so your tone comes through. When all is said and done, being the first to reach out isn't weakness, it's maturity, and they'll respect you for it.

title: Reconnecting With An Old Friend
prompt_text: Your friend recently came across the name of someone they were very close to years ago but slowly lost touch with. They would love to reconnect, but they feel awkward reaching out after so much time and worry the person might find it strange. Your friend asks you whether and how they should reach out. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Reassure your friend that reaching out is worth it, advise sending a light friendly message, give two reasons (people usually appreciate it, little to lose), add an example message, and close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Look, I'd say go for it, because honestly you'll regret it more if you don't reach out. Trust me, almost everyone loves getting a message from an old friend out of the blue, it can make their whole day. What I'd recommend is keeping that first message light and warm, nothing heavy at all. Something like, "Hey, your name popped up and it made me smile, it's been way too long, how have you been?" There are really two reasons not to overthink this. First, people rarely find a friendly hello strange, in fact most feel flattered that you thought of them after all these years. Besides, worst case they just take a while to reply, which costs you nothing, and best case you rebuild a friendship you genuinely miss. If they respond well, you could suggest a quick call or a coffee to properly catch up. At the end of the day, the people we drift apart from are usually still happy to hear from us. So send that message today, before you talk yourself out of it.

title: A Friend Who Is A Bad Influence
prompt_text: You have a long-time friend whose company you really enjoy, but lately they keep pushing you toward choices you are not comfortable with, like skipping responsibilities, overspending, or staying out far too late. You can feel it affecting your own habits and goals, but you do not want to lose the friendship or seem judgmental. Your friend has not changed, and you are not sure how to respond the next time it happens. What advice would you give about how to handle this friend?
template_hint: Name the tension honestly, advise setting firm but kind boundaries rather than cutting them off, give two reasons (protecting your goals, real friends respect limits), add an example of saying no, and close with self-respect.
sample_response_band12: The truth is, you don't have to choose between the friendship and your own goals, you just need some firm boundaries. Here's what I'd do. Keep enjoying their company, but get comfortable saying no without a big speech. Just, "I'm good tonight, I've got an early start," and leave it there. For one thing, you shouldn't have to justify protecting your time and money, a simple no is a full sentence. What's more, if you keep giving in, you'll slowly drift away from the habits and goals you actually care about, and that resentment will poison the friendship anyway. That said, pay attention to how they react. A real friend might tease you a little but ultimately respects your limits, whereas someone who keeps pressuring you after you've said no is showing you exactly who they are. Bottom line, you can love someone and still refuse to follow them down a path that isn't yours. Stay true to yourself, and the right people will stick around regardless.

title: A Friend Who Shares Your Secrets
prompt_text: You just found out that a close friend has been telling other people the private things you told them in confidence. You feel hurt and you are not sure whether to confront them, quietly pull away, or give them another chance. You still care about the friendship, but you do not feel you can trust them right now. What advice would you give someone in this situation about how to handle it?
vocabulary_tips: give the benefit of the doubt
template_hint: Validate the hurt, advise confronting them calmly before deciding anything, give two reasons (clarity and a fair chance), include an example of how to raise it, and close on protecting your trust going forward.
sample_response_band12: I hear you, finding out a secret got out really stings, and feeling betrayed is completely valid. But before you quietly pull away, if it were me, I'd actually talk to them first. Calmly say something like, "I shared that with you in confidence, and it got back to me from someone else, so I need to understand what happened." The main reason is clarity. You deserve to know whether it was a careless slip or a real pattern, and their reaction will tell you a lot, whether they own it or get defensive. Another thing, giving them one honest conversation means that whatever you decide afterward, you'll know you were fair and you won't second-guess yourself later. Even so, I wouldn't rush to fully trust them again. Rebuilding trust takes time, so going forward, just be more careful about what you share until they earn it back. All things considered, confront the issue, watch how they respond, and let their honesty, not your hurt, guide what you do next.

title: A One-Sided Friendship
prompt_text: A close friend confides in you that one of their friendships feels completely one-sided. They are always the one reaching out, making plans, and offering support, while the other person rarely puts in the same effort or shows up when needed. Your friend feels drained and underappreciated, and is unsure whether to address it or simply step back. They ask you what you think they should do. What advice would you give your friend?
connector_phrases: When it comes down to it
template_hint: Acknowledge your friend's exhaustion, advise them to test the friendship by pulling back a little and gently naming the imbalance, give two reasons (clarity and self-respect), add a concrete example of what they could say, and close by reassuring them they deserve mutual effort.
sample_response_band12: To be honest, the fact that you feel this drained is a real sign, and you shouldn't just brush it off. What I'd suggest is a two-part approach. First off, try gently naming it. You could say something like, "I really value our friendship, but lately I feel like I'm the only one reaching out, and I'd love it to feel more mutual." That gives them a fair chance to step up. On top of that, ease off a little and see what happens. Stop being the one to always text first, and notice whether they make any effort to close the gap. There are two reasons this works. One, it gives you clear information, because either they realize it and meet you halfway, or you finally see the effort was never really there. Two, it protects your own energy, since constantly pouring into someone who doesn't pour back leaves you empty for the people who do. Either way, you'll have your answer. When it comes down to it, you deserve friendships built on real give and take, so please don't feel guilty for expecting that.

title: Meeting Your Partner's Parents
prompt_text: Your younger cousin is going to meet their partner's parents for the very first time this weekend. They are really anxious about making a good first impression and not knowing what to say. Since you have been through this yourself, they have come to you for help. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Open by acknowledging the nerves, give one main piece of advice with a reason, add a second tip with a quick personal example, then close with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Hey, honestly, don't stress too much, everyone feels nervous about this. If I were you, the first thing I'd do is bring a small thoughtful gift, like flowers or a dessert. It instantly shows you care, and it gives everyone something to talk about when you walk in the door. On top of that, I'd suggest you ask them questions about themselves, where they grew up, how they met, that kind of thing. People love talking about their lives, and it takes the pressure off you to perform. When I met my partner's mom, I just asked about her garden, and we ended up chatting for an hour, she loved me after that. The main thing is, just relax and be yourself. They already know their kid likes you, so you're halfway there. Don't try to be perfect, that comes across as fake. At the end of the day, being warm, polite, and genuinely interested goes a long way. You've got this, trust me, you'll be totally fine.

title: Making Long-Distance Love Work
prompt_text: Your close friend just found out that their partner is moving to another city for a two-year job contract. They are worried the distance will ruin the relationship, and they don't know how couples cope with being so far apart. They turn to you and ask for your honest advice. What would you tell them?
connector_phrases: Another thing to keep in mind
At the end of the day
template_hint: Reassure that long-distance can work, give a practical communication tip with a reason, add a second tip about trust or visits with an example, then close encouragingly.
sample_response_band12: Okay, first of all, take a breath, long-distance is hard, but tons of couples make it work. Here's the thing, the most important advice I can give you is to set a regular routine for talking. Maybe a quick call every evening, even just ten minutes. It keeps you in each other's daily lives so you don't slowly drift apart. What really helps too is planning your visits in advance. Having a date circled on the calendar gives you both something to look forward to when things get lonely. My sister did long-distance for a year, and she said booking flights early was the only thing that kept her sane. Another thing to keep in mind is trust, you can't be checking up on each other constantly, that just creates stress. If you ask me, you have to give each other space and believe in the relationship. At the end of the day, two years isn't forever. If you both put in the effort, you'll come out the other side even stronger. You can absolutely do this.

title: Deciding To Move In Together
prompt_text: Your coworker has been dating someone for about a year, and their partner just suggested moving in together. Your coworker is excited but also a little nervous about whether it is the right time. They know you have lived with a partner before, so they come to you and ask for your advice. What advice would you give them?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Acknowledge the big decision, advise an honest conversation about expectations with a reason, add a tip about a trial period or finances with an example, then close supportively.
sample_response_band12: Oh, that's a big step, congrats! But I totally get why you're nervous. The way I see it, before anything else, you two need to sit down and have a really honest talk about expectations. Things like how you'll split the bills, who does the chores, how much alone time you each need. It sounds unromantic, but trust me, those little daily habits are what cause most arguments. On top of that, what I'd recommend is doing a trial run first, like spending a full week or two together before you sign a lease. When I moved in with my ex, we never tested it, and I had no idea he stayed up till three a.m. every night, and honestly it drove me crazy. So spend some real time together first. Just make sure you're not rushing into it because of pressure or convenience, do it because you genuinely want to build a life together. At the end of the day, if the foundation is strong, moving in will only bring you closer. You'll figure it out.

title: When a Partner Overworks
prompt_text: Your neighbour has been feeling lonely because their partner works extremely long hours and is barely ever home. They don't want to start a fight, but they feel like the relationship is suffering. They've come to you because they trust your judgment. What advice would you give your neighbour about dealing with a partner who works too much?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Validate the loneliness, advise raising it gently without blame and explain why, add a tip about protecting quality time with an example, then close with hope.
sample_response_band12: I'm really sorry, that sounds lonely, and your feelings are completely valid. Here's what I think, the most important thing is how you bring it up. Instead of attacking them or saying you work too much, try telling them how you feel, like I miss you and I want more time together. When people feel blamed, they get defensive, but when they feel loved, they actually listen. What might work better is suggesting one small, specific thing, maybe a regular date night or a phone-free dinner once a week. It's easier to agree to something concrete than a vague promise. My friend did this, she just asked for Sunday mornings together, and it completely changed their relationship. On top of that, keep in mind they might be stressed or close to burning out, so come at it with compassion, not an ultimatum. The key thing is you're a team trying to fix this together. At the end of the day, if you both care, you can find a better balance, so just start the conversation gently.

title: Calming First-Date Nerves
prompt_text: Your friend from your evening class has a first date tomorrow with someone they really like, and they're so nervous they can barely sleep. They keep worrying they'll say something awkward or run out of things to talk about. What advice would you give your friend about handling their nerves before the date?
connector_phrases: The best thing you can do is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Normalize the nerves, give one tip to reduce pressure with a reason, add a conversation tip with a quick example, then close with a confidence boost.
sample_response_band12: Aw, take a deep breath, being nervous just means you actually care, which is a good sign! Honestly, the best thing you can do is take the pressure off yourself. Don't think of it as some big test, think of it as just grabbing coffee with a cool new person. If it goes well, great, and if it doesn't, it's only one evening. That mindset alone will calm you down a ton. What I'd suggest is preparing a couple of easy questions to break the ice, like asking about their hobbies or their favourite places to eat. People love talking about themselves, so it keeps the conversation flowing and takes the spotlight off you. When I was nervous before a date once, I just asked about their dog, and we ended up talking for two hours. Plus, just keep in mind they're probably nervous too. At the end of the day, just be yourself, smile, and have fun. You're awesome, and they'd be lucky to spend time with you. Go enjoy it!

title: Introducing A Partner To Friends
prompt_text: Your sibling is about to introduce their new partner to their old group of friends, but they're worried everyone won't get along or that it'll feel awkward. They really want their friends and their partner to like each other, but they have no idea how to set up that first meeting. They've come to you for advice. What would you tell your sibling about introducing a new partner to old friends?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Reassure them, advise choosing a relaxed setting with a reason, add a tip about creating common ground with an example, then close on a positive note.
sample_response_band12: Oh, I totally get the worry, but honestly, don't overthink it. If I were you, I'd start by picking a really low-key setting for the first meeting, like grabbing food or playing board games, not some big, formal dinner. A relaxed vibe takes the pressure off everyone and makes it way easier to chat naturally. What also helps is giving your friends a little heads-up beforehand, maybe mention a shared interest, like, hey, she's really into hiking too. That way people already have something to talk about and they hit it off faster. On top of that, I'd keep this first hangout pretty short and casual. When I introduced my partner to my friends, we just did a quick coffee, and it broke the ice perfectly. Just remember, your friends want you to be happy, so they're already on your side. At the end of the day, if you keep it relaxed, they'll get along just fine.

title: Advice On Splitting Holidays With Family
prompt_text: Your close friend and their partner are arguing about whose family to spend the upcoming holidays with, since both families live far away and both expect a visit. Your friend is really stressed and doesn't want to upset anyone on either side. They know you've been through this yourself, so they're asking for your advice. What would you tell them about deciding whose family to spend the holidays with?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Empathize with the stress, suggest a fair system like alternating or splitting with a reason, add a tip on communicating early with a personal example, then close reassuringly.
sample_response_band12: Ugh, that's such a tough one, and honestly it's so common, you're not alone in feeling torn. Here's the thing, the fairest way to handle it is usually a system you both agree on. What I'd do is alternate years, so one family this year, the other next year. That way nobody feels like they're always losing out, and it takes the argument off the table for good. Another option is to split the holiday itself, like one family on the eve and the other on the day, if they're close enough to travel between. My cousin does the alternating thing, and it honestly ended years of fighting. More importantly, whatever you choose, tell both families early and frame it positively, so they have time to adjust and don't feel rejected. People handle disappointment way better when they're not blindsided. Whatever you decide, just make sure you and your partner present it as a united front, so neither set of parents can pressure one of you. At the end of the day, as long as it's fair and you communicate kindly, everyone will understand.

title: Helping a Friend Quit Vaping
prompt_text: Your close friend has been vaping for a couple of years and recently told you they really want to stop for good. They have tried quitting twice before but went back to it within a few days, and now they feel discouraged and unsure where to start. They know you care about them and trust your judgment. What advice would you give your friend about quitting vaping?
connector_phrases: The first thing I'd do is
At the end of the day
template_hint: Open warmly with empathy, give one main piece of advice, support it with two reasons or strategies plus a short concrete example, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Hey, first of all, I'm really proud of you for wanting to quit, and don't beat yourself up about the past attempts. Honestly, that's part of the process. If I were you, the first thing I'd do is figure out your triggers, like when you usually reach for it. For a lot of people it's after meals or when they're stressed, so try replacing that moment with something else, maybe chewing gum or going for a quick walk. What I'd also suggest is not going completely cold turkey on your own. Cravings hit hard, so consider talking to a pharmacist about patches or a quit-smoking app that tracks your progress. On top of that, tell a few people you trust so they can cheer you on. For example, my cousin quit by texting me every time he had a craving, and it really helped him stay accountable. At the end of the day, you've already done the hardest part by deciding. I'm here for you, so let's tackle this together, okay?

title: Advice on Cutting Back Sugar
prompt_text: Your younger cousin has noticed they feel tired all the time and want to cut back on how much sugar they eat, especially sweets and sugary drinks. They are not sure how to start without feeling deprived or giving up everything they enjoy. They have come to you because you eat fairly healthily. What advice would you give your cousin about cutting back on sugar?
template_hint: Greet kindly, give one clear, doable piece of advice, back it with two reasons and a relatable example, and finish with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Hey, I totally get how that feels, and the good news is you don't have to give up everything at once. The way I see it, going extreme just makes you crave sweets even more. If I were you, I'd start small by swapping your sugary drinks for water or sparkling water with a bit of lemon. Sodas and juices are loaded with hidden sugars, and they're a big reason you get that energy crash in the afternoon. What works for me is keeping fruit around, so when a craving hits, I grab an apple or some berries instead of candy. Another thing I'd suggest is reading labels, because sugar sneaks into things like cereal and sauces. For example, I switched my flavoured yogurt to plain with honey, and I barely noticed the difference. Trust me, after a couple of weeks your taste buds adjust and super-sweet stuff actually starts tasting too sweet. Just go easy on yourself and aim for moderation, not perfection. You've got this, cousin.

title: Reducing Daily Screen Time
prompt_text: Your roommate has realized they spend almost every free hour scrolling on their phone and watching videos, and it is starting to hurt their sleep and mood. They want to cut down a lot but say they reach for their phone without even thinking. They have asked you for some practical ideas. What advice would you give your roommate about reducing their screen time?
connector_phrases: At the end of the day
template_hint: Acknowledge the struggle, give a main strategy, support it with two practical tips and an example, then close with encouragement.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, it's great you noticed this, because most people don't even realize how much they scroll. Here's the thing: phones are designed to keep you hooked, so it's not really about willpower. If I were you, the first thing I'd do is turn off non-essential notifications, so you're not getting pulled back in every few minutes. One thing that helps is setting screen time limits in your settings, so the app actually nudges you when you've hit your cap. On top of that, try keeping your phone out of the bedroom and charging it across the room. Looking at screens late at night really messes with your sleep, which is probably why your mood feels off. For instance, I started reading a few pages of a book before bed instead of scrolling, and now I fall asleep way faster. At the end of the day, you don't have to quit cold turkey, just swap some of that time for something you enjoy offline. Want to try a screen-free hour together this week?

title: Easing Back Pain at Work
prompt_text: Your coworker sits at a desk all day and has started having constant lower back pain and bad posture, especially by the end of their shift. They are worried it is getting worse over time but do not want to spend a fortune on fancy equipment. They know you sorted out a similar problem last year. What advice would you give your coworker about fixing their back pain and posture?
template_hint: Show empathy, give one main fix, support it with two affordable strategies and a personal example, then close with reassurance.
sample_response_band12: Oh, I feel for you, because I dealt with the exact same thing last year and it's miserable. The key is that you don't need expensive gear to fix it. If I were you, the first thing I'd do is adjust your setup so your screen is at eye level and your feet are flat on the floor. What really helped me was stacking a couple of old books under my monitor and rolling up a small towel to support my lower back in the chair. It costs nothing. Another thing is to get up and move every half hour, even just to refill your water, because sitting still for hours is what stiffens everything up. On top of that, try a few gentle stretches in the morning and some core exercises a couple of times a week, since a stronger core takes pressure off your back. Trust me on this, within a few weeks I went from aching every evening to barely noticing it. If the pain sticks around though, definitely see a doctor. You'll feel so much better soon.
